cards.text: {{c1::Copy elision}} is a compiler optimization that avoids unnecessary {{c2::copying}} of objects, even when the copy constructor would normally be called.

cards.back: A form of copy elision where the compiler eliminates the copy when returning an object by value from a function

cards.question: What optimization occurs in this code?
cards.answer: RVO (copy elision). The compiler constructs w directly instead of creating a temporary, copying it, and destroying the temporary.
cards.explanation: Modern compilers optimize this to construct the object directly in the caller's space, avoiding copies entirely.

cards.front: What is the difference between copy initialization and direct initialization?
cards.back:
Copy initialization: Type obj = value; (uses =)
Direct initialization: Type obj{value}; or Type obj(value);
Direct initialization is preferred as it's more efficient and explicit.

cards.text: As of C++17, copy elision is {{c1::mandatory}} in certain cases (like returning a temporary), not just an optimization. These are called {{c2::guaranteed copy elision}}.

cards.concept: Why copy elision matters for performance
cards.explanation:
Copy elision eliminates expensive copy operations, especially important for:
  • Large objects (vectors, strings with lots of data)
  • Objects that manage resources (avoiding duplicate resource management)
  • Temporary objects in expressions
  • Return values from functions
Can significantly improve performance by eliminating unnecessary object construction and destruction.
examples: // Without elision: construct temp, copy to result, destroy temp
// With elision: construct directly into result location

cards.front: Does copy elision require the copy constructor to exist?
cards.back: In C++17+, guaranteed copy elision doesn't require a copy constructor. However, for non-guaranteed cases (where elision is optional), the copy constructor must still be accessible even if it's not called.

cards.question: Which initialization is preferred and why?
cards.code:
A) Widget w = Widget{5};

B) Widget w{5};
cards.answer: B is preferred. It's direct initialization - clearer, more concise, and doesn't rely on copy elision.
cards.explanation: Direct initialization (B) is the modern C++ way. Copy initialization (A) works but is unnecessarily verbose and conceptually involves copying (even if elided).

cards.text: Best practice: Use {{c1::direct initialization}} (braces or parentheses) instead of {{c2::copy initialization}} (equals sign) for class objects.

cards.front: What is NRVO and when might it not apply?
cards.back:
NRVO (Named Return Value Optimization) elides copies when returning a named local variable. It might not apply if:
  • Returning different objects from different code paths
  • The compiler can't prove only one object is returned
  • Optimization is disabled

cards.front: Should you rely on copy elision for program correctness?
cards.back: For C++17+ guaranteed copy elision, yes. For optional copy elision (NRVO), no - it's an optimization, not guaranteed. Your code should work correctly even if elision doesn't occur (except in guaranteed cases).
